title: America forgot how to make a classified nuclear warhead ingredient
author: Ellsworth Toohey
summary:
The U.S. government forgot how to make a classified component of its own nuclear warheads, then spent $92 million figuring it out again. The material is called Fogbank, and almost everything about it is classified — its composition, its purpose, and how it's manufactured. — Read the rest

The post
full_text:
The U.S. government forgot how to make a classified component of its own nuclear warheads, then spent $92 million figuring it out again. The material is called Fogbank, and almost everything about it is classified — its composition, its purpose, and how it's manufactured.

Fogbank is used in the W76, W78, and W88 nuclear warheads. It's believed to be an aerogel that gets superheated into plasma as part of the process that triggers the fusion stage of a thermonuclear explosion. A former manager at Oak Ridge National Laboratory described the situation this way: "The material is classified. Its composition is classified. Its use in the weapon is classified, and the process itself is classified."

The Y-12 plant in Oak Ridge, Tennessee, manufactured Fogbank from 1975 to 1989, then shut down that production line in 1993. When warhead refurbishment work began around 2000, the manufacturing records were sparse, and the people who knew the process had retired or died. The government first spent $23 million exploring alternatives, found none that worked, and then spent another $69 million successfully reproducing the original material — a process completed in 2008. By September 2007, the problem had been elevated to "Code Blue" priority. The W76 life-extension program, which upgraded 800 warheads using the rediscovered process, finished in December 2018.
